title: Chapter 10: The Gap Between Us
content: The coffee maker gurgles from across the kitchen like it's trying to clear its throat, and I follow the smell before I'm even awake enough to know I'm moving. That burnt-caramel scent hitting the back of my nose, the one that always makes my feet carry me to the counter on autopilot even when nothing else is running on autopilot anymore.
Leo's already there.

He's sitting on one of the stools with his phone propped against the espresso machine, screen glowing that particular blue-white that looks almost surgical against the morning dark. His shoulders are hunched forward the way they get when he's been in one position too long. His coffee is untouched beside him — no steam coming off it. Cold.
And his face.

He's been crying. I can tell even though he's already wiping his cheek with the back of his hand by the time I round the island. The redness around his eyes. The way his jaw is doing that locked-up thing. He heard my footsteps and he's putting himself back together in real time, slotting the pieces into place like I didn't just see them scattered all over the counter.
"Hey," he says. Voice rough. He clears his throat. "Hey. Coffee's ready."

"I can smell." I pull a mug from the cabinet — the one with the chip on the rim that I keep using anyway because everything in this penthouse is too perfect and that chip is the only real thing — and pour. The marble counter is cold under my forearms when I lean against it. Expensive cold. The kind that costs more per square foot than most people's rent.
He's still looking at his phone. Not hiding it, exactly. More like he got caught mid-thought and doesn't know what to do with his hands.
"What are you looking at?"
He hesitates. His thumb hovers over the screen. Then he turns it toward me.
Photos.

Not the kind I post. Not the curated grid with the right lighting and the right angle and the right version of my face that 11 million people have decided they want to see every day. These are the other kind. The ones that exist on someone else's camera roll, in someone else's memory, taking up storage space because the person who took them can't bring themselves to delete them.

Prom. Me in a dress I borrowed from my roommate because I couldn't afford one, standing in front of a CVS because the photo backdrop at the school was tacky, and Leo had said — what had he said? Something about how I looked like I was about to rob the place. I'm grinning in the photo. Not smiling. Grinning. The kind of grin that hurts your face.

Late-night ramen. The place near MIT that was open until 3 a.m. and smelled like MSG and ambition. I'm holding up a forkful of noodles, mid-slurp, eyes half-closed. There's broth on my chin. I don't care.

And then — the one that makes me stop breathing for a half-second — a candid. Leo must have taken it from across a room or someone else did. I'm laughing. Not the controlled, photogenic laugh I do on camera. The ugly one. The one where my mouth is too wide and my nose is scrunching up and I'm pretty sure I'm snorting. I'm snorting and I don't care and someone caught it on film and kept it.
I'm looking at myself and I feel — what do I feel?

Curiosity. That's the closest word. Like looking at a stranger who happens to wear my face. She's in these photos and she is so alive. So unselfconscious. So full of something that I can identify by its outline the way you can identify a country by its shape on a map without knowing anything about what it's like to live there.
"That's you," Leo says quietly. Like he's not sure I'll recognize her either.
"Yeah," I say. "That's me."
He puts the phone down and takes a breath and I can see him deciding something. Choosing to go further than he planned.
"You want to know what I think about?" he says. "When I can't sleep?"

I sip my coffee. It's bitter. Too much time in the pot, not enough sugar, the way I've been drinking it since the deduction because at least bitter is a taste and most things taste like nothing now. "Tell me."
"The fire escape. MIT. Junior year."

I don't say anything. I know about the fire escape. It's in the file of facts I carry around about my own life, the one that replaced whatever filing system my heart used to have. Junior year. Leo's dorm. The fire escape that overlooked the Charles River.
"You were wearing my hoodie," he says. "The gray one with the hole in the pocket, because yours got paint on it in the studio. You were doing that series — the abstraction stuff, with the —"
"Indigo and copper. I remember."

"You were wearing my hoodie and it was huge on you and you kept pulling the sleeves over your hands." He's not looking at me now. He's looking at the cold coffee. "It was October. Maybe forty degrees. The wind was coming off the river and it smelled like — I don't know. Mud and salt and that specific Cambridge smell, like old money and exhaust."
"I remember the smell," I say. I do. Factual. The Charles River at night. Brackish and cold.
"You leaned in first."
I didn't know that. Or maybe I did and it fell out with everything else.

"You leaned in first," he says again, and his voice cracks on the last word the way ice cracks when you step on a frozen puddle — not dramatic, just structural. Something giving way. "You put your hand on my face and you leaned in and you said —" He stops. Rubs his eyes with the heels of his hands. "You said, 'I've been wanting to do this for so long.'"

I stand there with my bitter coffee and I listen to him remember this and I know — I know the way I know the capital of France, the way I know that water freezes at 32 degrees, the way I know any fact that sits in my brain without touching any of the walls — I know that this is what love sounds like.
I know it the way you can identify a piece of music by reading the sheet notes without ever hearing it played.
He loved me. He loves me. That was a moment where two people decided to be brave at the same time and the world got smaller and warmer for exactly long enough to matter.

I can see the shape of it. I can catalog its features. Temperature, approximately forty degrees. Olfactory input, brackish river. Tactile, oversized hoodie sleeves over hands. Visual, the city lights reflecting off the water.

But I can't feel the echo of it. I can't find the part of me that was there. The memory is a photograph I've never been in. Someone handed me a picture of a stranger kissing someone she loves and said, "This is you," and I believe them, but believing isn't feeling and I don't know how to make it one.

"That's —" I start, and I don't know what comes next. Beautiful? Real? Important? All of those things are true the way that the sun is 93 million miles from Earth is true. Accurate and completely useless.
"That's a good memory," I settle on.
Leo nods. Picks up his cold coffee. Puts it down again without drinking it.
"Why are you still here?"

It comes out before I can sand the edges off it, and for a second I think I've said it wrong. Think I've made it sound like I want him gone. But that's not it. That's not it at all. I'm genuinely, factually, desperately confused.
He looks at me. That look he has where I can tell he's weighing about sixteen possible answers and discarding fifteen of them.

"I can't feel what you want me to feel," I say, because I need him to understand that this isn't a bit, isn't a test, isn't a trap. "I know I should. I know it mattered. I know that the girl on that fire escape leaned in first and said something that mattered. But it's gone, Leo. It's just — gone. Like someone took an eraser to the part of the brain that gives a shit and now I'm standing here with all the outlines and none of the color."
I expect him to flinch. To get that look people get when I say something honest in the wrong room, the one that says they wish I'd kept performing because the real thing is uncomfortable.
He doesn't flinch. He breathes. In through the nose, out through the mouth, the kind of breath you take before you say something you can't take back.
"Because you would have stayed for me."
I wait. That can't be the whole sentence.

"The you before all this." His jaw tightens. I can see the muscles working beneath the skin, the architecture of someone clenching against something that wants out. "She would have burned the world down for me. I owe her the same."
Oh.

That's — I don't have a word for what that is because the word I need is on the other side of the gap and I can't reach it. But I understand the shape of it. I understand that Leo Park is sitting at my kitchen counter with cold coffee and a red-rimmed face and he is not here because of who I am now. He's here because of who I was. And that's not the same thing as staying for someone, but it's also not the same thing as leaving, and I think maybe for him, right now, that distinction is the only thing keeping him in the chair.
"You owe her," I repeat.
"Yeah."
"Even though she's gone."
"Even though she's gone."
I should say something else. Something that matches the weight of what he just said. Something warm and grateful and broken in the way that would make this a moment worth photographing.
I say, "Okay."
And I watch something flicker across his face that I can identify — disappointment, that's the word — and then he nods and picks up his laptop and the conversation is over.
He works at the dining table. I can see him from the kitchen if I angle my head right, which I do, because there's nothing else to do and watching Leo code is at least a form of input.

His fingers move fast. Not the frantic fast of someone typing an essay at midnight, but the precise fast of someone who thinks in logic gates. Click-clack, click-clack. The keyboard sound is steady, almost meditative, and it fills the apartment the way white noise fills a room — you don't notice it until it stops.
I watch his hands and something happens.

Not an emotion. I need to be clear about that. Not the warm rush of affection that apparently used to hit me when I watched Leo do anything with his hands — dismantle a laptop, untangle a charger cable, crack his knuckles in that specific order he's been doing since middle school. Not that. But something.
A scene. A picture in my head that arrives fully formed, like a screenshot from a camera I didn't know I had.

Leo, twelve years old. His bedroom. The cluttered one with the model airplanes on the shelf and the poster of some band I never cared about. He's got a radio on his desk — an old one, the kind with the dial and the antenna and the speakers that crackle. He's taking it apart. His fingers are smaller but they move the same way. Fast, precise, sure. He's laid out the pieces in neat rows: screws here, circuit board there, speaker cone to the left. He's not going to break it. He's going to put it back together. He always puts things back together.

I have this memory. I'm looking at it right now. But I'm not in it — I'm watching from the doorway, and I can see the back of his head, the way his hair used to stick up at the crown because he hadn't figured out product yet.
It's factual. No warmth. No pull. No voice in my chest saying, I love this person. Just a clean, well-lit scene from a life I apparently lived.
But it's something.
It's more than I had an hour ago.
My phone buzzes on the counter and I glance at it without picking it up.

The notification is a milestone alert. Follower count. 12,000,043. The little graph next to it is a line going up and to the right, smooth and relentless, the kind of curve that would make a VC firm salivate.

Another buzz. 12.1M. The app wants me to know. It wants me to post. It wants me to lean into the momentum, capitalize on the growth, keep the algorithm fed and happy and producing. The machine doesn't know that Leo Park is sitting at my dining table with a heart that's cracking down the middle. The machine doesn't care about fire escapes or cold coffee or the specific way someone's jaw looks when they're holding back something that would drown them if they let it out.
The algorithm is still optimizing. Still growing. Still eating.
I put the phone face-down on the marble. The sound it makes is small and final, like closing a book you're tired of reading.

I look at Leo. His fingers are still moving. The keyboard is still clicking. He's deep in whatever he's building, the Mephisto thing, the thing he won't fully explain because he says I don't need to know yet, and I believe him because even without the warmth I can still recognize competence.
"Leo."
He doesn't look up. "Mm."
"I don't feel it." I say this clearly because I've learned that vagueness with Leo just makes him fill in the blanks with worst-case scenarios. "But I think I should. Does that count?"
He stops typing. His fingers hover over the keys for a second, and then they settle. He doesn't turn around.
"Ask me again when you feel it."

It's not cruel. That's the thing. If it were cruel, I could file it under antagonism and move on. It's not even dismissive. It's — tired. It's the voice of someone who has been holding up a wall with his bare hands and has just been asked if maybe someday he could hold up two walls.

I don't have a response. I drink my bitter coffee and I look at the back of his head and I think about the word "should" and whether that's the same as "want" and whether either of them is the same as "will."

That night, the apartment is the kind of quiet that has texture. The city is out there — I can hear it if I listen, the distant bass of a delivery truck, the hydraulic sigh of a bus kneeling at a stop three blocks away. But inside, it's just me and the dark and the gap.
I lie in bed and I try.
Not to sleep. To feel.

I start with Leo because Leo is the closest thing I have to a target. I run through the facts: he's smart, he's loyal, he stayed when he didn't have to, he remembers the temperature on the night we kissed. These are data points. They should add up to something. In a functioning system, they'd compile into warmth, into want, into the gravitational pull that makes one person orbit another.
My system isn't functioning.

So I zoom out. Not Leo specifically. Anyone. The idea of love. The concept of wanting someone so much that their absence rearranges the furniture in your chest. I've read about it. I've watched it in movies. I've posted about it, for God's sake — captions about how much I adore my people, heart emojis, little declarations that 11 million people double-tapped because they recognized the shape of the feeling even if I was just performing the outline.

I search for the feeling the way you search for a word on the tip of your tongue. You know it's there. You know it exists. You can almost taste the syllables. It's right there, behind the last door in the last hallway of the last room in the house of your brain, and if you could just — reach — a little further —
It moves.

Every time I reach for it, it moves. Like a cat that lets you get one inch from its fur before it shifts to the other side of the couch. Not running. Just — elsewhere. Adjacent. Present but inaccessible. I can feel that there's a space where the feeling should be. I can feel the edges of it. It has a shape. If I could name the shape, I'd be halfway there.

But I can't. And I'm lying in a bed that costs more than my childhood apartment, in a penthouse above the city, with a phone that's counting my worth in follower increments every few seconds, and the only person in the world who thinks I'm worth sticking around for is running out of patience with his own pain.

I know that because I saw it in his face when he said, "Ask me again when you feel it." The way he didn't turn around. The way his fingers kept moving on the keys even though his voice had gone hollow. He's not going to leave — he said so, he owes the old me too much — but staying isn't the same as surviving it, and I watched enough of my grandmother's decline to know what it looks like when love becomes a duty and duty becomes a cage.
The gap is widening.

I can feel that much. Not the thing on the other side, but the gap itself — the negative space, the cold air where connection used to live. I'm becoming more aware of what's missing even as I become less capable of finding it.

That's the part that might be the cruelest. Not the absence. The awareness of the absence. Someone with no taste buds doesn't mourn flavor. But I'm standing in the kitchen with a tongue that works and a meal I can't eat and every day the food is right there and every day it might as well be a photograph.
I close my eyes.

On the other side of the wall, I can hear Leo's keyboard. Click-click-click. He's still up. He's still working. He's still trying to fix something, because that's what he does — he takes things apart and puts them back together, radios and laptops and apparently one very broken girl who used to lean in first.
I hope he puts me back together.
I don't feel the hope. But I think it. And right now, thinking is the same distance from feeling as I am from the person in those photos on his phone.
Which is to say: close enough to see. Too far to touch.
